I remember that using bitwise operations can sometimes have unintended consequences, especially with negative numbers because Java uses two's complement for integers. So, when instant is a long and sum is also a long, their bitwise AND might not behave as expected in terms of overflow detection.

The XOR (^) operation helps detect sign changes because when you XOR two numbers of opposite signs, some bits will be set. Using AND might not capture this correctly because it would only check specific bits and not the overall change in sign.

Therefore, changing from (instant ^ sum) < 0 to (instant & sum) < 0 could prevent the overflow detection from working as intended, leading to incorrect behavior or exceptions not being thrown when they should be.
